original_abstract: In Batak Toba society, deeply ingrained patriarchal structures and the traditional kinship system of *Dalihan Na Tolu* continue to marginalize women, often relegating them to domestic roles (*parhobas*) and restricting their access to inheritance and public decision-making. This paper examines these gender disparities through the lens of Ivone Gebara’s feminist and liberation theology. By conducting a comprehensive literature review of Gebara’s work, the study explores her critique of androcentric religious frameworks and her advocacy for an inclusive, egalitarian interpretation of the divine. Gebara’s eco-feminist perspective—which emphasizes the maternal and nurturing attributes of the Spirit—is contextualized within Batak Toba culture, drawing parallels with the indigenous cosmological figure *Si Boru Deang Parujar*. The research suggests that integrating Gebara’s theological insights with local cultural symbols can provide a foundation for social transformation, fostering a public narrative that empowers women and promotes gender equality within the Batak Toba community. By bridging religious norms and grassroots social justice, this study offers a framework for more sustainable and inclusive gender relations in the Indonesian context.
humanized_abstract: It can be seen in many aspects of life of Batak Toba society. Men have a higher role while women have low social status, as they are made subjugate to men. According to Batak tradition which underlies the kinship system of *Dalihan Na Tolu*, women have domestic function (*parhobas*) and are deprived of inheritance rights and the right to take part in societal decision. In this study, the gender issue which still occurs in Batak Toba society will be studied using feminist and liberation theology by Ivone Gebara. It is developed by deeply analyzing a review of the writings of Gebara. Gebara analyses androcentric elements of religious systems which excludes the participation of women and attempts to develop an approach towards a gender justice through Spiritist doctrine interpreted by means of eco-feminist approach. According to her, the maternal and nurturing aspect of Spirit refers to The Divine as Mother. The study of the eco-feminist doctrine of Gebara and connecting the aspects in her doctrine with the cosmology figure *Si Boru Deang Parujar* from Batak culture, implies that the values as introduced by Gebara can be an approach and model of constructing the social change which is expected to foster women empowerment through the establishment of public discourse conducive for advancing gender justice. Therefore, this study will bring closer the relations between religious doctrine and the pursuit for social justice as means to realize gender justice which is required in this era.
